exemplar.title: M25 #4: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: M25 #4: Do the ever-improving tools of an area of knowledge always result in improved knowledge? Discuss with reference to two areas of knowledge.

strengths: Maintains a clear, sustained focus on the prescribed title throughout the essay.
Effectively uses two distinct areas of knowledge (natural sciences and the arts) with contemporary, concrete examples (wearable health devices, CRISPR, digital music streaming, AI-generated art).
Demonstrates awareness of different points of view by acknowledging benefits and drawbacks (ethical breaches, data privacy concerns, questions of artistic integrity).
Provides relevant real-world case studies (e.g., Jason M. Allen, CRISPR) that anchor abstract discussion in familiar contexts.
weaknesses: Discussion remains largely descriptive, offering limited critical analysis of how tools affect reliability, scope, or nature of knowledge.
Key TOK concepts (justification, reliability, perspective, ways of knowing) are seldom named or explored, reducing depth of inquiry.
Counter-claims and ethical implications are noted but not fully evaluated or linked back to broader knowledge questions.
Argumentative coherence is occasionally disrupted by abrupt shifts in perspective and incomplete or repetitive sentences, undermining the flow of the analysis.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The studentâs essay clearly addresses the prescribed title and provides a structured comparison of two areas of knowledge with a range of relevant, contemporary examples. This positioning aligns with the upper end of Band 3â4 in the TOK rubric: the discussion is focused and supported by examples, and there is some awareness of alternative viewpoints. However, the treatment of knowledge questions remains predominantly descriptive and lacks the sustained, nuanced evaluation and use of TOK terminology required for higher bands. A final mark of 4 accurately reflects this level of achievement.

annotations.comment: The thesis that tools may not always improve knowledge is stated, yet criteria for âimprovementâ remain vague, limiting clarity on how arguments will be evaluated.
The instruction to reference two areas of knowledge is clearly echoed, providing a solid structural roadmap for the discussion.
The shift between patient and researcher perspectives is abrupt, leading to an unclear evaluation of whose knowledge is improved or harmed.
The studentâs essay title precisely matches the prescribed question, establishing immediate focus and ensuring relevance to the TOK prompt.

Definition of âtoolsâ is descriptive and sourced from a dictionary, but the student misses an opportunity to link this definition to TOK concepts such as justification, reliability or perspective.
Use of the Jason M. Allen case provides a compelling real-world conflict that illustrates tensions in knowledge production within the arts.
An incomplete sentence at the end of the AI art discussion interrupts the argument; the idea requires completion and deeper exploration.
The choice of natural sciences and the arts is apt, but the justification for selecting these areas of knowledge is not articulated, reducing the essayâs critical grounding.
The wearable health device example is well described with clear reference to real-world impact on data collection, illustrating contemporary relevance.
Despite rich description, there is little direct analysis of how wearable technology affects the reliability or scope of healthcare knowledgeâkey TOK considerations are missing.
Mention of TikTok and user curation demonstrates awareness of evolving knowledge pathways in the arts, connecting tools and discovery.
The blanket statement that more music quantity does not equate to better knowledge lacks supporting justification or counter-examples.
Ethical concerns about data privacy are raised, but their implications for the justification and credibility of knowledge claims are not developed.
The use of Fitbit as a concrete example strengthens the discussion by anchoring abstract concerns in a familiar technology.
The CRISPR case study is detailed and relevant, showcasing both groundbreaking potential and ethical hazards effectively.
The discussion notes ethical fears but does not critically evaluate how such constraints alter the scope or production of scientific knowledge.
There is little use of TOK languageâterms like âknowledge frameworks,â âways of knowing,â or âjustificationâ are absent, reducing analytical depth.
The claim that fear prevents scientific inquiry is asserted without supporting evidence or exploration of counterarguments within the scientific community.
The arts section introduces digital music appropriately, identifying a clear shift from physical formats to streaming platforms.
While accessibility is discussed, the essay fails to examine how streaming affects the reliability or depth of artistic knowledgeâkey TOK dimensions.
The concluding sentence of the wearable tech section is repetitive and disrupts flow; tighter transitions would enhance coherence.
Calling for tools to be used âwiselyâ lacks specificity; the student should outline criteria or principles guiding responsible tool use in knowledge production.
The discussion of AI-generated art is current, but lacks evaluation of how algorithmic creativity influences the nature and credibility of artistic knowledge.
The conclusion restates the thesis generally but offers no new evaluation of implications or reflection on how knowledge communities might adapt.

The essay does not
reach a standard
described by levels
1â5 or is not a
response to one of
the prescribed titles
on the list for the
current session.
The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of
knowledge.
Arguments are
offered and are
supported by
examples.
There is some
awareness of
different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
